---
title: "Research"
page-layout: article
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
title-block-style: none
---

<style>
#title-block-header {
  display: none;
}
</style>

```{=html}
<div style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#F39C12,#E05A4E);color:white;padding:3rem 1.5rem 2rem;text-align:center;margin-bottom:2.5rem;border-radius:0 0 1rem 1rem;">
  <h1 style="font-family:'DM Serif Display',serif;font-size:2.2rem;font-weight:400;margin-bottom:0.4rem;">Research</h1>
  <p style="opacity:0.88;font-size:1rem;margin:0;">Convergent biology · Translational oncology · Health equity</p>
</div>
```

## Psychedelic Neuroplasticity and the Neurotrophic Architecture of Pancreatic Cancer (Convergent Pathways) {#pdac}

::: {style="display:flex; gap:0.5rem; flex-wrap:wrap; margin-bottom:1.5rem;"}
<span class="skill-badge ml">In Progress · 2026</span>
:::

### Overview

Psilocybin-assisted therapy is entering oncology settings at scale: clinical trials now enrol patients with cancer-related psychological distress, and advancing Phase 3 data for treatment-resistant depression have accelerated its broader integration into cancer care. Its therapeutic mechanism has been substantially reframed by Moliner *et al.* (2023), who demonstrated that psilocin, the active metabolite of psilocybin, directly binds the TrkB receptor as a positive allosteric modulator, positioning neurotrophin signalling as the primary driver of psychedelic-induced neuroplasticity.

Concurrently, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), a malignancy with near-universal perineural invasion (PNI) and a 12-month median survival post-diagnosis, is driven at the neural interface by nerve growth factor (NGF) signalling through TrkA, TrkB's closest structural paralogue, sharing approximately 60% intracellular kinase domain identity and activating identical downstream effectors: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/Akt, and PLCγ. Critically, TrkB is also overexpressed in PDAC tumour cells, where it promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition and anoikis resistance.

This paper maps the structural and signalling convergence of TrkA and TrkB; reviews the emergence of cancer neuroscience as a field; traces psilocybin's clinical trajectory in oncology populations; and proposes organoid-based neuro-tumour chip models and generative AI-driven pathway simulation as the minimum preclinical infrastructure required to characterise this interface before it is encountered in clinical practice.

---

### Computational Validation: TCGA-PAAD Analysis

To provide empirical grounding for the molecular hypothesis, I analysed *NTRK1* (TrkA) and *NTRK2* (TrkB) expression across the TCGA-PAAD cohort (n = 182 pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma tumour samples).

#### Expression of NTRK1 and NTRK2 in PDAC

Both neurotrophin receptors are expressed across the full PDAC cohort, with *NTRK1* showing a higher median expression (0.95 log₂ units) relative to *NTRK2* (0.32 log₂ units). The right-skewed distribution of *NTRK1* indicates a subset of tumours with very high TrkA expression, consistent with its established role in driving perineural invasion.

```{=html}
<figure>
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CarolGachema/TCGA-PAAD-NTRK/main/figures/Figure1_expression_distribution.png"
     class="img-fluid rounded"
     alt="NTRK1 and NTRK2 expression distributions in TCGA-PAAD"
     style="box-shadow:0 4px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.1); max-width:680px; display:block; margin:1rem auto;">
<figcaption>Figure 1. NTRK1 and NTRK2 expression across 182 PDAC tumour samples (TCGA-PAAD, HiSeqV2). Violin plots with embedded boxplot and individual data points. Median values annotated.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

#### Co-expression of NTRK1 and NTRK2

A significant positive correlation exists between *NTRK1* and *NTRK2* expression across PDAC tumours (Spearman ρ = 0.416, p < 0.001). This co-expression pattern establishes that both receptors are simultaneously active within the same tumour microenvironmental context — a prerequisite for the cross-talk the paper proposes.

```{=html}
<figure>
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CarolGachema/TCGA-PAAD-NTRK/main/figures/Figure2_coexpression_scatter.png"
     class="img-fluid rounded"
     alt="NTRK1 vs NTRK2 co-expression scatter"
     style="box-shadow:0 4px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.1); max-width:620px; display:block; margin:1rem auto;">
<figcaption>Figure 2. NTRK1–NTRK2 co-expression in TCGA-PAAD. Each point represents one tumour sample. Spearman ρ = 0.416, p &lt; 0.001. Labelled samples: top 6 co-expressing tumours.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

#### Survival Analysis

Kaplan-Meier analysis did not demonstrate a statistically significant association between *NTRK2* expression and overall survival at median-split dichotomisation (HR = 0.89, 95% CI 0.59–1.33, log-rank p = 0.57), nor for *NTRK1* (HR = 0.75, 95% CI 0.50–1.13, p = 0.17). 

This null finding is consistent with the uniformly poor prognosis of PDAC across molecular subtypes and the limited statistical power of n = 182 for detecting moderate gene-level effects. The paper's central argument is mechanistic rather than prognostic.

#### Pathway Enrichment

GSEA-KEGG analysis, with genes ranked by Spearman correlation with *NTRK2* expression across all TCGA-PAAD samples, identified **MAPK signalling** and **PI3K-Akt signalling** as significantly positively enriched pathways in the NTRK2 co-expression network — precisely the downstream effectors shared between TrkA (perineural invasion) and TrkB (psilocin target).

```{=html}
<div class="row g-3 my-2">
  <div class="col-md-6">
    <figure>
      <img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CarolGachema/TCGA-PAAD-NTRK/main/figures/Figure4_enrichplot_hsa04010.png"
           class="img-fluid rounded" alt="MAPK signalling pathway enrichment"
           style="box-shadow:0 4px 16px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);">
      <figcaption>MAPK Signalling Pathway (hsa04010) — positively enriched in NTRK2 co-expression network</figcaption>
    </figure>
  </div>
  <div class="col-md-6">
    <figure>
      <img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CarolGachema/TCGA-PAAD-NTRK/main/figures/Figure4_enrichplot_hsa04151.png"
           class="img-fluid rounded" alt="PI3K-Akt signalling pathway enrichment"
           style="box-shadow:0 4px 16px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);">
      <figcaption>PI3K-Akt Signalling Pathway (hsa04151) — positively enriched in NTRK2 co-expression network</figcaption>
    </figure>
  </div>
</div>
```

[📂 View full analysis on GitHub](https://github.com/CarolGachema/TCGA-PAAD-NTRK){.card-link} &nbsp; · &nbsp; Data: [UCSC Xena Browser](https://xenabrowser.net){.card-link}

---


## Why the HIV Stem Cell Cure Is Nearly Irrelevant for Sub-Saharan Africa {#hiv}

::: {style="display:flex; gap:0.5rem; flex-wrap:wrap; margin-bottom:1.5rem;"}
<span class="skill-badge soft">WHO dir</span>
<span class="skill-badge bio">2026</span>
:::

### Overview

The paper is a structural and bio-ethical critique of the current global HIV cure paradigm. It argues that while the scientific community celebrates allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) as a landmark proof of concept, this “cure” is a biological and logistical illusion for Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA), the region that bears over two-thirds of the global HIV burden. The core thesis is that the cure is built on an accidental, Euro centric genetic framework that completely excludes, underfunds, and ignores the populations that need a HIV cure the most

### The Problem
1. **Complete Absence of the Allele** - The cure relies entirely on a donor possessing the homozygous CCR5Δ32 genetic mutation, which stops HIV from entering host cells.
- While this mutation exists in about 1% of Northern Europeans, large-scale genomic data shows its frequency is precisely 0% in studied populations across Angola, Nigeria, Zimbabwe, and Ethiopia.The HLA Matching Compound Problem:
- Because successful transplants require strict Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA) tissue matching to prevent fatal graft-versus-host disease, an African patient cannot simply use a European donor.

2. **The Cost Wall** - A single allo-HSCT procedure costs between $100,000 and $300,000. The procedure demands specialized sterile bone marrow units, advanced blood banking, high-resolution HLA typing tech, and intensive care units equipped for extreme toxicities. These resources are fundamentally absent or severely constrained in most Sub-Saharan healthcare systems.

3. **The Cancer Requirement** - This procedure has never been a standalone treatment for HIV; it is an incredibly aggressive, last-resort intervention for patients who also have life-threatening blood cancers (like leukemia).

### Key Recommendations
The paper makes the following recommendations directed at the WHO and trial sponsors:

1. **Commission an SSA** - Focused Cure Agenda: Shift funding to study CCR5-independent remission mechanisms and invest in gene therapies with low-income access guidelines built into the initial trial phases.

2. **Diversify Global Donor Registries** - Launch targeted, well-funded recruitment drives for African, Asian, and Latin American donors to fix the massive European bias in global HLA matching data.

3. **Establish Regional Centers of Excellence** - Fund the construction of at least five regional advanced hematological and gene therapy medical hubs across Sub-Saharan Africa to serve both cure research and broader oncological needs.

4. **Enforce Advance Market Commitments (AMCs)** - Mandate tiered licensing agreements guaranteeing that when a viable, population-agnostic cure is found, it is affordable and accessible to LMICs immediately upon regulatory approval, preventing the multi-decade delay that occurred with early antiretroviral drugs.

---

## Neuropathological Consequences of Emerging Viral Pathogens in East Africa: {#cns}

::: {style="display:flex; gap:0.5rem; flex-wrap:wrap; margin-bottom:1.5rem;"}
<span class="skill-badge ml">IGAE Conference · 2026</span>
:::

### Overview
Emerging and re-emerging viral diseases pose a growing public health challenge in East Africa, yet much of the scientific focus has centered on acute infection and mortality rather than their long-term effects on the nervous system. 
This review explores how viral pathogens can affect the central nervous system through mechanisms such as neuroinflammation, blood-brain barrier disruption, immune-mediated injury, and direct viral invasion, resulting in neurological complications that may persist long after the initial infection has resolved.

Drawing on current evidence from the literature, the paper synthesizes the neuropathological mechanisms associated with emerging viral infections and highlights the implications for healthcare systems in East Africa. It identifies critical knowledge gaps in neurological surveillance, diagnosis, and long-term patient management while emphasizing the need for greater investment in neuroscience research within the region. 
The work reflects my broader interest in understanding disease mechanisms at both the molecular and systems levels, an interest that later informed my computational genomics projects.

###The Problem
- Limited understanding of the long-term neurological consequences of emerging viral infections in East African populations.
- Insufficient neurological surveillance and research infrastructure to monitor post-infectious neurological complications.
- A lack of region-specific evidence to guide clinical management, public health policy, and preparedness for future outbreaks.

### Conference Presentation
Abstract presented at the **Insitute for Global Academic Excellence Conference, 2026**.

---

```{=html}
<div class="contact-strip">
  <h3>Interested in collaborating?</h3>
  <p>Open to research partnerships, joint publications, and conversations about African health equity and computational biology</p>
  <a href="mailto:gachemacarol@gmail.com" class="contact-link">✉ gachemacarol@gmail.com</a>
</div>
```
